episode_meta.title: Rethinking the journalism business model in the age of AI

guests.name: Jeff Jarvis
guests.role: Co-host of This Week in Google and AI Inside; Author
guests.organization: Newmark School of Journalism at CUNY

use_cases.description: OpenAI licensed Axel Springer (owner of Business Insider) content to embed news within Microsoft Copilot and ChatGPT products
use_cases.outcome: Axel Springer content available within AI chat products; raises questions about intermediary licensing when Business Insider rewrites other outlets' stories

use_cases.description: Translation of foreign news content — Jeff Jarvis reads Helsingin Sanomat (Finnish newspaper) using Google Translate
use_cases.outcome: Able to read news in Finnish, one of the most difficult languages, expanding informational horizons

use_cases.description: Proposed crowdsourced journalism: editor asks 100 readers to record school board meetings, machine transcribes them, then queries the corpus to find patterns (e.g., how many boards are banning books vs. discussing student mental welfare)
use_cases.tool_used: Large language model with restricted corpus
use_cases.outcome: Would enable analysis at scale that an individual journalist could not perform

use_cases.description: Google Notebook LM used to summarize and query a restricted corpus of documents, helping journalists with research
use_cases.outcome: Useful for summarizing restricted document corpora; editorially directed by Steven Johnson

use_cases.description: Schibsted built an LLM-powered interface on top of their product review site (their version of Engadget), allowing users to query reviews conversationally (e.g., specifying TV size, room brightness, thinness)
use_cases.outcome: Users get personalized product recommendations from review content with cited sources; reliable because of constrained corpus

use_cases.description: Norwegian initiative to create a Norwegian-language LLM with publishers contributing their digitized content, led in part by Schibsted's CTO urging publishers at Nordic Media Day
use_cases.outcome: Research phase underway with publishers cooperating to build competitive Nordic-language models; commercial phase to follow

use_cases.description: CNET, GeoMedia, and Gannett used AI to generate articles at scale, resulting in brand damage due to errors and lack of transparency
use_cases.outcome: Negative — damaged credibility; Gannett covered high school sports games via machine but failed to disclose AI authorship to audiences

use_cases.description: Lawyer Steven Schwartz used ChatGPT to generate legal case citations that turned out to be fabricated, leading to a show-cause hearing
use_cases.outcome: Fabricated citations submitted to court; judge focused on attorney's professional responsibility failure rather than blaming the technology itself

use_cases.description: Jeff Jarvis uses dictation on Chromebook when injured wrist prevented typing, enabled by AI-powered natural language processing
use_cases.outcome: Able to continue working despite physical inability to type

use_cases.description: Leo Laporte explored the GPT Store, including building a Lisp programming GPT
use_cases.organization: Twit Network / This Week in Google
use_cases.outcome: Demonstrated GPT Store functionality; also discovered unauthorized 'girlfriend/boyfriend' chatbots violating platform rules

use_cases.description: Proposed idea: news organizations build a query interface (like Notebook LM) on top of their content and archives so readers can ask questions instead of only browsing editor-curated homepages
use_cases.organization: Hypothetical — proposed for New York Times and other news organizations
use_cases.outcome: Would provide personalized, conversational access to news archives; better than search or homepage curation

key_claims.claim: If journalism still thinks it is valuable only because it makes content, it is doomed. AI is not the agent of doom, but it shows us that future.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: The news industry is incredibly inefficient because it devotes a huge amount of resource to copying each other, each outlet rewriting the same stories to get their own page views and ad impressions.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: AI can raise the floor (enabling people who can't write/code/draw to do so), scale ability (do things faster and more), and raise the ceiling (do things previously impossible like protein folding or analyzing 100 school board meetings).
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (citing WEF discussion group)

key_claims.claim: On the negative side, AI will have uneven economic impact, enables scaling of evil, and robs some people of their sense of purpose.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (citing WEF discussion group)

key_claims.claim: When a large language model deals with a restricted corpus of information and cites sources, it is pretty good, reliable, and useful — unlike asking a raw model open-ended questions.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: Schibsted is smarter than any news company out there — more innovative, more strategic, more bold, and more successful in everything including paywalls and collaboration.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: Generative AI should not be used right now to write articles at scale because of hallucination — it is irresponsible.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: The most powerful programming language on planet Earth today is English.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (quoting unnamed CEO of an AI company from AI Inside podcast)

key_claims.claim: Media have turned the internet into an enemy and are now doing the same thing with AI, driven by moral panic.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: If LLMs had been positioned as fiction/creativity/brainstorming machines rather than super search engines, many of the current problems (hallucination concerns, misuse) would not exist.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

key_claims.claim: Norway is far ahead because all their newspapers and almost all books are already digitized, making it easier to build language-specific LLMs.
key_claims.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (citing Norwegian AI executive)

challenges.description: News content loses value when it can be freely copied digitally and linked to — you cannot control distribution the way you could with physical media like CDs
AI-generated content at scale (CNET, GeoMedia, Gannett) damaged publisher credibility when not properly disclosed to audiences
Hallucination in generative AI makes it irresponsible for writing news articles at scale
Uneven economic impact of AI — some people lose their sense of purpose (writers, coders); impact distributed unevenly across society
AI enables the scaling of evil — bad actors can do harmful things at much greater scale
Labor issues arise from AI-generated illustration displacing human illustrators, similar to how the camera displaced earlier forms of visual art

Dangerous philosophies driving some AI leaders — techno-creationism, transhumanism, long-termism, effective altruism — which prioritize hypothetical future humans over present ones and concentrate decision-making power among technologists
Media coverage of AI is too simplistic, swinging between utopia and dystopia, failing to provide nuanced understanding
English-language dominance in LLMs means non-English languages need dedicated models (motivating the Norwegian LLM initiative)
Transitioning from research phase to commercial phase of collaborative LLM efforts could break down cooperation when publishers negotiate individual deals

quotes.text: If we still think that we're valuable only because we make this thing called content, we are doomed. And AI is not the agent of doom, but it shows us that future.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

quotes.text: I didn't know the machine could lie, he said. Which is somewhat defensible, right? You know, that it's presented as a super search engine he said. That's Microsoft's mistake.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (recounting lawyer Schwartz's defense)

quotes.text: AI can raise the floor. It can enable people to do things they otherwise couldn't do. People who can't write can write. People who can't code can code... It also scales ability... It also raises the ceiling and lets us do things that we weren't otherwise capable of doing, like folding proteins or analyzing 100 school board meetings.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (citing WEF discussion)
quotes.topic: AI capabilities framework: floor, scale, ceiling

quotes.text: Schibsted's view is let's beat him. I spoke to the board of directors of Schibsted one year and I tried to tell them what they should do in cooperation with Google and the CTO at the time, different guy, said we're better than Google.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis
quotes.topic: Schibsted's competitive approach to AI vs. litigious approaches

quotes.text: Imagine if when LLMs came out, instead of saying that they were this super search engine or this way to query the world, what if they'd been positioned as fiction machines? As creativity machines, a lot of the problems that we put on them would go away.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis
quotes.topic: Framing and positioning of LLM capabilities

quotes.text: The judge interrupted and said, I set out to do no such thing. He didn't care about the technology. He cared about its misuse by attorneys who didn't do their full job. Same will be said of journalists if they use this to cheat.
quotes.speaker: Jeff Jarvis (recounting judge's statement in Schwartz hearing)
quotes.topic: Professional responsibility when using AI tools

recommendations.advice: News organizations should build LLM-powered query interfaces on top of their own content and archives, allowing readers to conversationally search for personalized information instead of relying solely on editor-curated homepages or traditional search.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Publishers should cooperate to build language-specific LLMs (as Schibsted is doing with Norwegian/Nordic languages) rather than suing or passing laws against tech companies.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: If using AI to generate content (e.g., high school sports coverage), always disclose AI authorship to the audience and invite corrections — transparency preserves brand trust.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Journalism must reevaluate and reinvent itself around different spheres of value beyond content production — including conversation, community engagement, and new user experiences.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Journalism education should teach prompt writing as a critical skill, help students understand they can now program machines without being coders, and train them on both opportunities and limitations of AI models.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Journalism education should incorporate humanities and social sciences — anthropology, psychology, ethics, community studies, design, and history — to prepare students as leaders who understand the internet and AI as human networks, not just technology.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Newsrooms should use AI to enable sources to tell their own stories and to illustrate those stories, rather than relying solely on traditional journalist-mediated narratives.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis

recommendations.advice: Use LLMs with restricted/constrained corpora for reliable results — when the model works with a specific set of documents and cites sources, it is much more reliable than open-ended querying.
recommendations.speaker: Jeff Jarvis
